data.title: DON: The Dividend Focus Doesn't Justify The Higher Fees
data.description:
The WisdomTree U.S. MidCap Dividend Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:DON), launched on 06/16/2006 by WisdomTree Inc. and co-managed by WisdomTree Asset Management, Inc. and Mellon Investments Corporation, provides dividend-focused exposure to the mid-cap segment of the U.S. equity market. It charges 0.38% as an expense ratio and currently manages about $3.8 billion.

In other articles, I have expressed my opinion about dividend-focused ETFs these days; I consider them defensive, and due to the current macro context, I generally favor them. "Generally" is the keyword because in this case, mid-cap exposure is defensive on its own, without having to rely on other factors. Both reasoning and evidence support the thesis that mid-cap exposure is adequate because of its simplicity, the lower fees involved, and better diversification.

Portfolio

DON tracks the WisdomTree U.S. MidCap Dividend Index, making it a unique product in that it offers access to dividend-paying midcap stocks. Generally, you don't see this combination.

Now, it doesn't just select dividend-paying stocks within the mid-cap segment. It weights the constituents based on the size of their cash dividends instead of market cap. This is what results in a meaningfully different portfolio than what mid-cap ETFs usually offer.

As a result of fewer holdings than those of such other funds, the top 10 holdings represent 10% of the portfolio. For context, the top 10 holdings of the SPDR S&P MIDCAP 400 ETF Trust (MDY) account for 6.8% of the fund.

A significant difference can be observed in the sector composition. Financials make up one quarter of the fund. In MDY, the sector accounts for about 17%. This increases the macrosensitivity when it comes to DON's performance.

Speaking of which, this is the evidence that I was referring to above. DON's returns are consistent with its more concentrated portfolio.

Performance

By looking over the long-term return differential between DON, MDY, and the S&P 500, you can see that it has lagged the market more recently (as expected), but more importantly, it has slightly underperformed MDY.

The differential may not appear that significant if you're a dividend investor, but it begs the question of why you should pay higher fees for similar and potentially worse performance in some periods (MDY charges an expense ratio of 0.24%).

As I said before, DON is unique as far as this combination of factors is concerned. Therefore, its closest peer is the ProShares S&P MidCap 400 Dividend Aristocrats ETF (REGL), which focuses on dividend growth stocks instead of simply dividend-paying ones and equal-weights. Naturally, REGL has been consistently outperforming it in the long run. Other pure mid-cap peers have also done better, again questioning how warranted the higher expense ratio is here.

Now, contrary to other defensive options, DON didn't perform better than the market on a risk-adjusted basis, YTD. Its drawdown as the tariff narrative unfolded a few months back is similar in severity.

Speaking of tariffs, let's take a look at one key challenge DON is likely to face in the future.

Outlook

DON, like all U.S. equities in general, is likely to see slower earnings growth as a result of a slower pace in economic growth. Economists forecast a much slower YoY rate in GDP change for the next couple of quarters as a result of the Trump administration's trade policy changes. This might be temporary as implied by the longer-term median predictions, which are closer to recent GDP growth levels, but it could result in an opportunity risk for investors who are not underweight equities when earnings results finally reflect the impact of tariffs.

The trajectory of the nonfarm payroll growth this year suggests that the labor market is slowly softening, which should support the case for slower economic growth.

The last Jobs Report may have reassured investors regarding the state of the labor market, and yet it's anything but promising if you look closer. The U.S. economy added 147K jobs in June, which was admittedly much higher than the economists' prediction of 110K. This increase over the previous month, however, lacked the breadth required to claim that the labor market's cooling is more of a supposition than a fact.

Growth has been concentrated in healthcare and state/local government jobs. More precisely, half of the jobs added were government jobs and healthcare, leisure and hospitality, and construction jobs accounted for 36% of the private jobs. Two thirds of private-sector jobs witnessed an aggregate 20K decline.

In other words, the reliance on a few industries for the trend's potential reversal is problematic and may result in a head fake very soon.

And yet, the market is optimistic. Fund managers (NAAIM index) and retail investors (AAII index) are reallocating to levels seen at the start of the year before the tariff narrative increased uncertainty and volatility.

Such positioning doesn't seem to account for the growth risks presented by tariffs in the mid term. Being underweight or using defensive options are warranted. Nevertheless, investors should be mindful of how efficient such options are. Both dividend investing and mid-cap exposure can be used to express a defensive stance, but the combination doesn't seem like it's worth the extra fees.

Your Turn

Now, I am always open to challenging my views because being an ideologue in investing and trading can prove expensive. So if you have a different opinion, then this platform is the right place to discuss both strategy and the various options with which we can position our portfolios. Go ahead and leave a comment below to let me know what you think.

Otherwise, please let me know if you found this article useful. It means a lot!

Thank you for reading.

data.title: PMTV: With Q2 Financials Released, This Bond Stands Out
data.description:
Thesis

We have noticed a significant debate on the Seeking Alpha platform regarding the validity of common equity investments in certain mortgage REITs, and the alternative of using preferred shares. We would also like to point out that retail investors have bonds available to undertake mortgage REIT risk, and we are going to explore such a choice.

PennyMac Mortgage Investment Trust (PMT) announced its results for Q2 2025 on July 23, 2025. The REIT was up in price after the announcement, with the market happy with the forward guidance.

In today's article we are going to analyze the PennyMac Mortgage Investment Trust NT 30 (NYSE:PMTV) in light of the Q2 2025 financials, and the trading levels for the debt versus the preferred shares for the REIT. We will show investors why we like the PMT balance sheet, and why we favor debt over preferred equity here.

A solid Q2 2025, with some over-hedging

PMT is one of the few mortgage REITs to display a more stable book value over time:

In the past decade the REIT's price is down -25%, versus almost -50% for the rest of the cohort. The REIT actually provides for a snapshot for the past two years:

Please note the very stable book value, and the net generation of diluted EPS per quarter. Also note that return on equity ('ROE') becomes constrained only when rates experience a high level of volatility like in H1 2025.

There are two main reasons for this performance:

  • mortgage servicing rights (MSRs) present on the balance sheet which act like a hedge
  • lower mark to market leverage (i.e., more non-recourse debt via securitizations)

Mortgage servicing rights are the contractual obligations and benefits a company receives for managing a mortgage loan on behalf of the loan's owner or investor. When a mortgage is originated, the lender may sell the loan to an investor but retain the right to "service" the loan. When interest rates go up, for example, the average life of a mortgage loan goes up, thus providing for a longer stream of cash benefits from MSRs. PMT has a significant amount of MSRs:

As you can see from their Q2 income statement, MSR income exceeds the one generated by leveraging up Agency MBS bonds ($34mm vs $21mm). The two products also serve as a hedge to each other:

The yellow bars above pertain to MSR fair value changes, whereas the blue bars are the MBS book of business fair value changes. Kindly note they always move in opposite directions.

The company also generates cash via retaining the equity portion of Agency securitizations, namely the GSE credit risk transfer line. Which brings us to the second point, namely non-recourse leverage:

PMT goes about leveraging the balance sheet without the need for hedges. When doing a securitization and retaining the equity piece, the hedging part is not really needed anymore, with the company running credit risk. As per the latest financials that portfolio is doing very well fundamentally:

GSE credit risk transfer securities are at the bottom of the stack in an Agency securitization, and represents actual credit risk if there are issues:

GSEs bundle mortgages into mortgage-backed securities and guarantee the principal and interest payments to MBS investors. CRT securities isolate the credit risk (risk of default) from these mortgage pools and transfer it to private investors, such as institutional investors or reinsurers, through structured financial transactions. Investors in CRT securities receive a recurring fee (akin to an insurance premium) in exchange for bearing potential losses if defaults in the mortgage pool exceed a certain threshold.

So if we look at the above table from PMT, we can see that the portfolio runs a high FICO score of 753, has an ever decreasing LTV (currently 43%), and a fairly stable delinquency profile. Please keep in mind that as time passes by, these portfolios naturally de-risk themselves because homeowners keep paying interest and principal, which reduces the LTV further.

New baby bond issuance

The company has taken a liking in baby bond issuances, and in February 2025 it issued new notes:

WESTLAKE VILLAGE, Calif.--(BUSINESS WIRE)--PennyMac Mortgage Investment Trust (NYSE: PMT) (the “Company” or “PMT”) today announced that it has priced an underwritten public offering of $150,000,000 aggregate principal amount of its 9.00% Senior Notes due 2030 (the “Notes”).

The notes are currently trading at $25.36/share and come with an 8.85% current yield and a 8.6% yield to maturity. The notes are senior unsecured obligations of the company, and are to be used to redeem some of the 2026 debt and for general corporate purposes.

We tend to like senior unsecured debt for REITs better than preferred shares because of the set maturity date. If one looks at the Series A preferred shares (PMT.PR.A) from the company, they will notice they are trading at a 8.4% current yield. The two are very similar, yet PMTV has a defined maturity date and an ever decreasing risk profile.

Also to note that the refinancing profile for the name is fairly heavy:

Given the debt maturity wall for 2026 and 2027, we do not anticipate any sort of preferred share early redemption. The company needs to refinance debt and wait for much lower interest rates before thinking about the preferred shares. And that is precisely why we like PMTV.

Why we like PMTV

There are several reasons to like the bonds:

  • seniority over preferred shares
  • defined maturity date
  • ability to benefit from lower Fed Funds later this year and in 2026
  • debentures from a company with a stable book value for its sector

Kindly note that mortgage REIT debt will always yield more than standard corporate issues because of the highly leveraged balance sheets the REITs run. Although the coupon is comparable to junk debt, PMT's long operating history tells us they have been successful at managing their interest rate risk and credit risk in different market cycles.

Conclusion

PMT is a mortgage REIT that has shown the ability to maintain a stable book value. The company does this using MSR as a natural hedge to its Agency MBS book. Although the REIT did some over-hedging in the past quarter, its book value has remained stable. PMT is still a fairly leveraged entity, but uses securitization equity tranches to run risk rather than mark to market facilities. The GSE risk transfer book of business is stable, with attractive risk analytics. Its 2030 notes PMTV are trading with a high current yield of 8.85%, and offer better risk/rewards when compared to the preferred shares.

data.title: Google Giveth, Tesla Taketh Away
data.description:
Markets drifted into Thursday’s close like a lazy canoe ride - just enough movement to keep things interesting, but not enough to get wet. Megatech pulled in opposite directions, leaving the rest of the market paddling in circles with salad forks.

Level Change 7/24/25 (%)

-0.7 Dow

+0.2 Nasdaq

+0.3 Nasdaq 100

+0.1 S&P 500

-0.9 S&P 400

-1.6 S&P 600

The S&P 500 and Nasdaq managed fresh all-time highs by the skin of their megacap teeth, though participation was light and enthusiasm lighter. Equal-weight indexes sagged, with small caps stumbling over rising yields and declining relevance.

Google (GOOG +0.9%) did its part to keep the AI dream alive - and well-funded.

Its Q2 report served up hearty beats on revenue, profit, and cloud growth, proof that sprinkling AI jargon over a very non-AI business model can still fool almost everyone. Booming ad sales and YouTube’s transformation from cat-video depot to living room staple added the cherry. The formula is simple: let ads do the lifting while AI takes the bow.

That Google plans to burn through $85B this year to stay in the AI arms race didn’t so much as ruffle a hair on its fan base. The real business funds the AI habit, and investors seem happy to foot the bill.

Then there’s Tesla (TSLA -8.2%), the AI company that still moonlights in car manufacturing.

Shares skidded more than 8% after CEO Elon Musk warned of a few challenging quarters, which, in Musk-speak, could mean anything from tighter margins to colonizing Europa. Car sales fell, regulatory credit revenue plunged, and investors - already acclimated to the smell of scorched expectations - reached for the Febreze.

The Republican budget law scraps EV incentives, something Musk initially waved off, until analysts pegged the cost to Tesla of up to $1B a year. Now, he’s paying attention.

Chinese competitors steadily improve on an already strong base, while Tesla’s earnings trajectory now resembles a Cybertruck panel: oddly shaped and full of dents. Musk tried to redirect attention to robotaxis, which are either the future of transport or a Philip K. Dick subplot, depending on your appetite for faith-based investing.

If Google’s results showed that chasing AI dreams doesn’t preclude turning a profit, Tesla’s reminded us that eccentricity and politics don’t always mix well.

Google executes, Tesla promises. Romantic Wall Streeters can’t resist a good story, but even romance has earnings season.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Fidelity International Small Cap Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fidelity International Small Cap Fund (MUTF:FISMX)

Performance Review

For the three months ending June 30, 2025, the fund's Retail Class shares gained 13.99%, trailing the 17.02% advance of the benchmark MSCI (MSCI) All Country World Index ex U.S. Small Cap Index.

International small-cap stocks performed well in the second quarter, following a similar trajectory to that of U.S. markets. Global equities were sent into a brief freefall in early April by President Trump's announcement of a sweeping tariff plan. However, stocks pulled out of their nosedive and rallied strongly into quarter end, as the Trump administration paused most tariffs.

Looking at the major regions within the benchmark, the U.K. led the pack with a 22% gain. Canada and Europe ex U.K. were next, each climbing about 19%, followed by Asia Pacific ex Japan (+18%), and emerging markets (+177%). Only Japan (+12) underperformed the broader benchmark. By sector, the growth-oriented information technology and communication services (+21% each) sectors were the top performers in Q2, mostly reflecting the potential for generative artificial intelligence to drive transformative change.Elsewhere, industrials and consumer discretionary stocks, which typically move in step with the economy, rose 21% and 15%, respectively. Financials (+20%) also fared well. The biggest laggard proved to be consumer staples firms, which were up 11%.

Turning to the fund, security selection in the U.K., positioning in Asia Pacific ex Japan- especially Israel- as well as investment choices in Europe ex U.K., notably detracted. Within emerging markets, ,exposure to South Korea and China also hurt. Sector-wise, picks in industrials were the biggest challenge, followed by financials. The portfolio's modest cash position pressured performance in what was a strong upward market.

On a stock-specific basis, an overweight in Ashtead (AHSTF) Technology Holdings (-111%) detracted most this quarter. The company is a leading provider of equipment rentals, advanced underwater technologies and support services to the global offshore energy industry. The worldwide transition toward offshore wind projects provides more of a long-term structural growth element, compared to traditional cyclical offshore oil and gas projects, thus helping to upgrade the quality of the business, in our view. With that said, a decline in the price of crude oil was a headwind for energy services companies the past three months. We capitalized on recent weakness to add to the position, making the stock one of the fund's larger holdings on June 30.

Out-of-benchmark exposure to Renesas Electronics (OTCPK:RNECF) (-66%) also hurt. As Japan's leading analog semiconductor maker, the firm is a major player in the industrial and automotive markets it serves. Moreover, recent acquisitions and internal developments have enabled the business to transform itself into a leader in power products. This period, though, the stock was overshadowed by other chipmakers with more direct ties to Al.

Conversely, positioning in Japan, Taiwan and Saudi Arabia contributed to performance versus the benchmark. Amongsectors, ,picks in health care and an underweight in real estate modestly helped.

Leading the way among individual holdings was an outsized position in TestResearch (+566%), a Taiwan-listed company specializing in manufacturing assembled circuit board automatic test equipment. The global surge in demand for advanced semiconductors, particularly those used in generative artificial intelligence applications, provided a favorable backdrop for the company's products.

An out-of-benchmark holding in Germany-based Rheinmetall (OTCPK:RNMBF) (+49%) also lifted the portfolio's relative return. The stock's strength carried over from Q1, when German election results and concrete steps taken to increase military spending in that nation drove robust gains.Rheinmetall, the fund's No.13 holding at quarter end- has been a standout performer since reaching a single-digit low in March 2020, as the COVID-19 pandemic gripped the world.

Outlook and Positioning

Since the fourth quarter of 2024, we've become somewhat more optimistic about the trajectory of the global economy. Moreover, we think international small-cap equities look attractive compared with the U.S. In particular, Europe ex U.K. appears quite promising, given the burst of new spending occurring there. Consequently, the fund continues to carry an overweight both there and in the U.K. as of June 30.

In emerging markets, the portfolio has a smaller-than-benchmark allocation, mostly because of India, South Korea and Taiwan.Elsewhere, China's economy continues to be problematic, but the government is pulling various levers to provide ongoing stimulus.With these thoughts in mind, we are positioning the fund somewhat less defensively.

FUND INFORMATION

Manager(s):

David Jenkins

Trading Symbol:

FISMX

Start Date:

September 18, 2002}

Size(in millions):

$5,180.48

Morningstar Category:

Fund Foreign Small/Mid Blend

Stock markets, especially foreign markets, are volatile and can decline significantly in response to adverse issuer, political, regulatory, market,or economic developments. Foreign securities are subject to interest rate, currency exchange rate, economic, and political risks, all of which are magnified in emerging markets. The securities of smaller, less well-known companies car be more volatile than those of larger companies.

Investment Approach

● Fidelity International Small Cap Fund is an opportunistic international small-cap strategy focused on our best ideas across geographies. The fund seeks capital appreciation.

● We favor higher-quality small-cap companies trading at a discount to theirintrinsic (fairr) value because we believe this combination can lead to outperformance over the course of a market cycle.

● This is a value-oriented approach where the fund looks to optimize the risk/reward trade-off by investing at the intersection of value and quality. Investing in "undiscovered" or "out of favor" securities is the key, in our view, to finding attractive valuations for "quality" businesses.

● Typically, businesses we own require less capital to grow, which leads to strong free cash flow.The combination of strong free cash flow and an unencumbered balance sheet allows astute management teams the flexibility to deploy capital for inorganic (acquisition-driven) growth or returns to shareholders.

Definitions and Important Information

Unless otherwise expressly disclosed to you in writing, the information provided in this material is for educational purposes only. Any viewpoints expressed by Fidelity are not intended to be used as a primary basis for your investment decisions and are based on facts and circumstances at the point in time they are made and are not particular to you.Accordingly, nothing in this material constitutes impartial investment advice or advice in a fiduciary capacity, as defined or under the Employee Retirement Income Security Act of 1974 or the Internal Revenue Code of 1986, both as amended. Fidelity and its representatives may have a conflict of interest in the products or services mentioned in this material because they have a financial interest in the products or services and may receive compensation, directly or indirectly, in connection with the management, distribution, and/or servicing of these products or services, including Fidelity funds, certain third-party funds and products, and certain investment services. Before making any investment decisions, you should take into account all of the particular facts and circumstances of your or your client's individual situation and reach out to a professional adviser, if applicable.

CHARACTERISTICS

Earnings-Per-Share Growth Trailing measures the growth in reported earnings per share over trailing one- and five-year periods.

Earnings-Per-ShareGrowth (IBESS 1-Year Forecast) measures the growth in reported earnings per share as estimated by Wall Street analysts.

Median Market Cap identifies the median market capitalization of the portfolio or benchmark as determined by the underlying security market caps.

Price-toBook (PP/B) Ratio is the ratio of a company's current share price to reported accumulated profits and capital.

Price/Cash Flow is the ratio of a company's current share price to its trailing 12-months cash flow per share.

Price-to-Earnings (P/E) Ratio (IBES 1-Year Forecast) is the ratio of a company's current share price to Wall Street analysts' estimates of earnings.

Price-to-Earnings (P/E) Ratio Trailing is the ratio of a company's current share price to its trailing 12-months earnings per share.

Return on Equity (ROE) 5-Year Trailing is the ratio of a company's last five years historical profitability to its shareholders' equity.Preferred stock is included as part of each company's net worth.

Sales-Per-Share Growth measures the growth in reported sales over the specified past time period.

Weighted Average Market Cap identifies the market capitalization of the average equity holding as determined by the dollars invested in the portfolio or benchmark.

Weighted Median Market Cap identifies the market capitalization of the median equity holding as determined by the dollars invested in the portfolio or benchmark.

IMPORTANT FUND INFORMATION

Relative positioning data presented in this commentary is based on the fund's primary benchmark (INDEX) unless a secondary benchmark is provided to assess performance.

INDICES

It is not possible to invest directly in an index. All indices represented are unmanaged. All indices include reinvestment of dividends and interest income unless otherwise noted.

Fidelity International Small Cap Fund Linked Index represents the performance of the MSCIACWI (Alll Country World Index) ex USA Small Cap Index since April 1, 2014, and the MSCI EAFE Small Cap Index prior to that date.

MSCI All Country World ex USA Small Cap Net MA (1-Apr-2014)Linked Index represents the performance of the MSCI All Country World ex US Small Cap (Net MA) since April 01, 2014, and MSCI All Country World Small Cap ex US (GroSs) prior to that date.

MARKET-SEGMENT WEIGHTS

Market-segment weights illustrate examples of sectors or industries in which the fund may invest, and may not be representative of the fund's current or future investments. They should not be construed or used as a recommendation for any sector or industry.

RANKING INFORMATION

©2025 Morningstar, Inc.All rights reserved.The Morningstar information containedherein: (1) is proprietary to Morningstar and/or its content providers; (2) may not be copied or redistributed; and (3) is not warranted to be accurate, complete or timely. Neither Morningstar nor its content providers are responsible for any damages or losses arising from any use of this information. Fidelity does not review the Morningstar data and, for mutual fund performance, you should check the fund's current prospectus for the most up-to-date information concerning applicable loads, fees and expenses.

% Rank in Morningstar Category is the fund's total-return percentile rank relative to all funds that have the same Morningstar Category. The highest (or most favorable) percentile rank is 1 and the lowest (or least favorable) percentile rank is 100. The top-performing fund in a category will always receive a rank of 1%. % Rank in Morningstar Category is based on total returns which include reinvested dividends and capital gains, if any, and exclude sales charges. Multiple share classes of a fund have a common portfolio but impose different expense structures.

RELATIVE WEIGHTS

Relative weights represents the % of fund assets in a particular market segment, asset class or credit quality relative to the benchmark. A positive number represents an overweight, and a negative number is an underweight. The fund's benchmark is listed immediately under the fund name in the Performance Summary.

3-YEAR RISK/RETURN STATISTICS

Beta is a measure of the volatility of a fund relative to its benchmark index. A beta greater (leSs) than 1 is more (leSs) volatile than the index.

Information Ratio measures a fund's activereturn (the fund's average monthly return minus the benchmark's average monthly return) in relation to the volatility of its active returns.

R-Squared measures how a fund's performance correlates with a benchmark index's performance and shows what portion of it can be explained by the performance of the overall market/index. R-Squared ranges from 0, meaning no correlation, to 1, meaning perfect correlation. An R-Squared value of less than 0.5 indicates that annualized alpha and beta are not reliable performance statistics.

Sharpe Ratio is a measure of historical risk-adjusted performance.It is calculated by dividing the fund's excess returns (the fund's average annual return for the period minus the 3-month "risk free" return rate) and dividing it by the standard deviation of the fund's returns. The higher the ratio, the better the fund's return per unit of risk.The three month "risk free" rate used is the 90-day Treasury Bill rate.

Standard Deviation is a statistical measurement of the dispersion of a fund's return over a specified time period. Fidelity calculates standard deviations by comparing a fund's monthly returns to its average monthly return over a 36-month period, and then annualizes the number. Investors may examine historical standard deviation in conjunction with historical returns to decide whether a fund's volatility would have been acceptable given the returns it would have produced. A higher standard deviation indicates a wider dispersion of past returns and thus greater historical volatility. Standard deviation does not indicate how the fund actually performed, but merely indicates the volatility of its returns over time.

Tracking Error is the divergence between the price behavior of a position or a portfolio and the price behavior of abenchmark, creating an unexpected profit or loss.

Fidelity

Before investing in any mutual fund, please carefully consider the investment objectives, risks, charges, and expenses. For this and other information, call or write Fidelity for a free prospectus or, if available, a summary prospectus. Read it carefully before you invest.

data.title: Fidelity International Real Estate Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fidelity International Real Estate Fund (MUTF:FIREX)

International real estate stocks gained 16.21% according to the FTSE EPRASM/NAREIT index. A broad-based rally in global stocks contributed to the outperformance of international real estate stocks during the quarter, partly attributable to rising foreign currencies versus the U.S. dollar. Monetary easing also provided a tailwind, as many non-U.S. central banks continued to cut interest rates, including the eurozone and U.K. Meanwhile, China saw signs of economic improvement through a pick-up in industrial activity and regulatory policy. Other positive factors included generally favorably supply/demand dynamics in some international real estate markets and idiosyncratic tailwinds, such as supply constraints and continued excitement around artificial intelligence, boosting demand for data centers.

Still, the backdrop was mixed. U.S. government policy uncertainty, especially around tariffs, remained high, and much of the actual and potential impact to economic and profit growth, inflation and bond yields is still ahead. Meanwhile, U.S. federal government funding cuts, layoffs and rising uncertainty led to a decline in consumer sentiment and slower spending.

Among the regional components of the FTSE® EPRASM/NAREIT® index,Europe notched the highest result, advancing roughly 21%.This region was boosted by double-digit gains from some of its largest real estate markets, including Germany (+34%), Spain (+26%), Belgium (+22%) and Switzerland (+21%). Germany, in particular, benefited from its second consecutive quarter of rising real estate prices following a slump that began in 2022. In April, the country loosened a regulatory measure that was established to curb its property crisis, signaling the country was more positive on the market. Outside of Europe, the U.K.(+16%) performed about in line with the broader asset class in the quarter.

Asia ex Japan gained 17%, propelled by Australia (+20%), the largest component of the region, and Hong Kong (+19%). The Reserve Bank of Australia cut interest rates in May- it's second cut this year. Hong Kong was boosted by regulatory tailwinds and continued investment from mainland China. Meanwhile, Japan, the largest country weight in the index, underperformed the FTSE index during the past three months, gaining about 9%.

Performance Review

For the quarter, the fund's Retail Class shares gained 13.85%, underperforming the sector index but outperforming the 11.95%}$$gain of the broad-market MSCI (MSCI) EAFE Index.

By country, security selection and an underweight in Australia weighed on the fund's relative performance the most. This included a sizable underweight in Goodman Group (OTC:GMGSF). Goodman is only one of only two data centers in Australia. The stock advanced roughly 27% this period due to the surging demand for data centers driven by Al.We exited the position in Goodman this quarter, as we preferred opportunities we thought could provide better long-term growth.

It also hurt to avoid index component Vonovia (OTCPK:VONOY) (+36%), a German residential real estate company.The business is highly levered and, as such, not a good fit for our investment strategy. This decision hurt during Q2 as the stock rose along with the German property market.Another notable relative detractor in Q2 was an overweight in Nomura Real Estate Holding (OTCPK:NMEHF) (+1%), a Japanese real estate holding company. Nomura- along with the broader Japanese REIT market-faced several macro headwinds, including trade policy uncertainty and concerns over the potential for rising interest rates. Nomura was a top-15 holding on June 30. A cash position of about 2%, on average, during the quarter also weighed on relative results given the strong market backdrop.

Conversely, stock picking and an underweight in Japan was helpful this quarter. We avoided Japanese property company and index component Mitsui Fudosan (OTCPK:MTSFY) (+9%), which lagged the index. A sizable out-of-benchmark position in Katitas (+32%) also contributed to the fund's relative result. While the company missed its full-year 2025 financial results, the firm forecasted strong revenue growth for its fiscal year ending March 31, 2026, and over the next three years. It also helped to own Inclusio, a Belgian property stock not held in the index. Shares of Inclusio gained 36% over the past three months, boosted by the strong regional macro backdrop.

LARGEST CONTRIBUTORS VS. BENCHMARK

Holding Market Segment Average Relative Weight Relative Contribution (basis points)*
Mitsui Fudosan Co Ltd Diversified Real Estate Activities -4.28% 32
Katitas Co Ltd (OTCPK:KTITF) Real Estate Development 2.02% 29
Inclusio SA Real Estate Operating Companies 1.42% 26

*1 basis point=0.01%.

LARGEST DETRACTORS VS. BENCHMARK

Holding Market Segment Average Relative Weight Relative Contribution (basis points)*
Vonovia SE Real Estate
Vonovia SE Operating Companies -3.69% -64
Goodman Group unit Industrial REITs -4.53% -50
Nomura Real Estate Holdings Inc Diversified Real Estate Activities 2.52% -41
Ingenia (OTC:INGEF) Communities Group unit Multi-Family Residential REITs 3.74% -37
Parkway Life Real Estate Investment Trust Health Care REITs 2.60% -30
*1 basis point=0.01%.

Outlook and Positioning

The end of June is marked by geopolitical and trade policies, which may pose headwinds for real estate groups such as industrial and retail, which should be more sensitive to tariffs and supply chain disruptions. Meanwhile, inflation expectations vary, particularly given the policy uncertainties, especially involving U.S. tariffs, which would drive up the global price of goods. If tariffs materialize, the threat of stagflation" is a significant one for the real estate sector.Therefore, despite the strong performance of some firms with highly levered balance sheets so far this year, we believe these stocks are unlikely to maintain this performance over the medium term.

Conversely, we believe stocks of companies with quality balance sheets are priced attractively. Along with better operating companies, we plan to continue to look for companies with better balance sheets, as we believe the reward from leverage is not adequate relative to the risk.

In addition, new requirements for CO2 emissions and the potential for lower spatial demand related to technological advances threaten to constrain the asset class. It's important to recognize that not all obsolete real estate assets are the same. While an obsolete office in the West End of London has viable residential rezoning opportunity, a data center in northern Norway may not. As a result, we believe residual value of real estate will be a more critical consideration over the next decade.

Another important reminder is that the sector's growth rarely stays well above inflation unless there are significant barriers to entry in a space or an under-tapped non-commodity service to that growth.Just because something is currently structurally undersupplied does not mean that will be the case in a decade. However, we believe there is a growing moat in many real estate markets, as green initiatives plus a "not-in-my-backyard" set of policies is driving land scarcity for a previously zero-barrier-to-entry sector. Thus, we believe the recovery in logistics may be more significant than investors are currently pricing in.

We also plan to keep a close watch over the next few years on residential undersupply, especially versus the pressure for regulation and rent control. We'll also continue to look for ways to involve the fund in property technology, a category in its infancy with largely unpenetrated addressable markets. One example is online residential property brokerage, which may disintermediate traditional residential brokerage. There are also opportunities in construction technologies, as the world looks for more efficient and more rapid construction techniques. The role of data and the regulation of data by country may facilitate or hinder further digitalization of real estate services. Another crucial aspect will be the integration of artificial intelligence into the strategies and operations of real estate companies. The companies that implement Al property tech solutions first may have a significant advantage over competitors. We intend to focus a substantial amount of our research efforts on this area, which the market appears to be overlooking.

FUND INFORMATION

Manager(s):

$$ \text{ Guillermo de las Casas} $$

Trading Symbol:

FIREX

Start Date:

September 08, 2004

Size(in millions):

$331.92

Morningstar Category:

Fund Global Real Estate

Stock markets, especially foreign markets, are volatile and can decline significantly in response to adverse issuer, political, regulatory, market, or economic developments. Foreign securities are subject to interest rate,currency exchange rate, economic, and political risks, all of which are magnified in emerging markets. Changes in real estate values or economic downturns can have a significant negative effect on issuers in the real estate industry. The value of securities of issuers in the real estate industry can be affected by changes in real estate values and rental income, property taxes, interest rates, tax and regulatory requirements, and the management skill and creditworthiness of the issuer. The fund may have additional volatility because it can invest a significant portion of assets in securities of a small number of individual issuers.

Investment Approach

● Fidelity International Real Estate Fund seeks capital appreciation through investments in securities of companies principally engaged in the real estate industry.The fund invests primarily in non-U.S. securities.

● Our philosophy is that owning stocks where the market is not pricing in the companies' long-term growth potential can lead to outperformance. Given the cyclicality and short-term nature that typically characterizes international real estate investing, we look for stocks that are less reliant on short-term factors and more driven by secular-growth business models. These qualities can often be underpriced by the market. We seek these stocks using a bottom-up approach with a long-term value bias, which is based on a blend of cash-flow and net-asset-value models.

● We believe that strong risk management is as important as stock selection, and therefore seek to limit foreign exchange risk, country risk, leverage risk and active sector exposure. The fund favors stocks with lower valuation multiples, lower leverage and higher long-term growth versus the benchmark.

CHARACTERISTICS

Earnings-Per-Share Growth Trailing measures the growth in reported earnings per share over trailing one- and five-year periods.

Earnings-Per-Share Growth (IBES 1-Year Forecast) measures the growth in reported earnings per share as estimated by Wall Street analysts.

Median Market Cap identifies the median market capitalization of the portfolio or benchmark as determined by the underlying security market caps.

Return on Equity (ROE) 5-Year Trailing is the ratio of a company's last five years historical profitability to its shareholders' equity.Preferred stock is included as part of each company's net worth.

Sales-Per-Share Growth measures the growth in reported sales over the specified past time period.

Weighted Average Market Cap identifies the market capitalization of the average equity holding as determined by the dollars invested in the portfolio or benchmark.

INDICES

Weighted Median Market Cap identifies the market capitalization of the median equity holding as determined by the dollars invested in the portfolio or benchmark.

INDICES

It is not possible to invest directly in an index. All indices represented are unmanaged. All indices include reinvestment of dividends and interest income unless otherwise noted.

FTSE EPRA/NAREIT Developed Ex North America Index(GroSs)

is a free float-adjusted market-capitalization-weighted index of all eligible developed-market real estate stocks worldwide excluding North America.

MSCI EAFE Index (Net MA Tax) is a market-capitalization-weighted index that is designed to measure the investable equity market performance for global investors in developed markets, excluding the U.S.& Canada. Index returns are adjusted for tax withholding rates applicable to U.S.-based mutual funds organized as Massachusetts business trusts (NR).

MARKET-SEGMENT WEIGHTS

Market-segment weights illustrate examples of sectors or industries in which the fund may invest, and may not be representative of the fund's current or future investments. They should not be construed or used as a recommendation for any sector or industry.

RANKING INFORMATION

©2025 Morningstar, Inc. All rights reserved.The Morningstar information contained herein: (1) is proprietary to Morningstar and/or its content providers; (2) may not be copied or redistributed; and (3) is not warranted to be accurate, complete or timely. Neither Morningstar nor its content providers are responsible for any damages or losses arising from any use of this information. Fidelity does not review the Morningstar data and, for mutual fund performance, you should check the fund's current prospectus for the most up-to-date information concerning applicable loads, fees and expenses.

% Rank in Morningstar Category is the fund's total-return percentile rank relative to all funds that have the same Morningstar Category.The highest (or most favorable) percentile rank is 1 and the lowest (or least favorable) percentile rank is 100.The top-performing fund in a category will always receive a rank of 1%. %Rank in Morningstar Category is based on total returns which include reinvested dividends and capital gains, if any, and exclude sales charges. Multiple share classes of a fund have a common portfolio but impose different expense structures.

RELATIVE WEIGHTS

Relative weights represents the % of fund assets in a particular market segment, asset class or credit quality relative to the benchmark. A positive number represents an overweight, and a negative number is an underweight. The fund's benchmark is listed immediately under the fund name in the Performance Summary.

IMPORTANT FUND INFORMATION

Relative positioning data presented in this commentary is based on the fund's primary benchmark (INDEX) unless a secondary benchmark is provided to assess performance.

3-YEAR RISK/RETURN STATISTICS

Beta is a measure of the volatility of a fund relative to its benchmark index. A beta greater (leSs) than 1 is more (leSs) volatile than the index.

Information Ratio measures a fund's active return (the fund's average monthly return minus the benchmark's average monthly return) in relation to the volatility of its active returns.

R-Squared measures how a fund's performance correlates with a benchmark index's performance and shows what portion of it can be explained by the performance of the overall market/index.R-Squared ranges from 0, meaning no correlation, to 1, meaning perfect correlation. An R-Squared value of less than 0.5 indicates that annualized alpha and beta are not reliable performance statistics.

Sharpe Ratio is a measure of historical risk-adjusted performance.It is calculated by dividing the fund's excess returns (the fund's average annual return for the period minus the 3-month "risk free" return rate) and dividing it by the standard deviation of the fund's returns. The higher the ratio, the better the fund's return per unit of risk.The three month "risk free" rate used is the 90-day Treasury Bill rate.

Standard Deviation is a statistical measurement of the dispersion of a fund's return over a specified time period. Fidelity calculates standard deviations by comparing a fund's monthly returns to its average monthly return over a 36-month period, and then annualizes the number. Investors may examine historical standard deviation in conjunction with historical returns to decide whether a fund's volatility would have been acceptable given the returns it would have produced. A higher standard deviation indicates a wider dispersion of past returns and thus greater historical volatility. Standard deviation does not indicate how the fund actually performed, but merely indicates the volatility of its returns over time.

Tracking Error is the divergence between the price behavior of a position or a portfolio and the price behavior of a benchmark, creating an unexpected profit or loss.
